title: Inventory Turnover Calculator
meta_description: Calculate inventory turnover from cost of goods sold and average inventory, and see days inventory outstanding. Formula and worked example included.

inputs.label: Cost of goods sold
inputs.help: Cost of goods sold over the period (usually a year).

inputs.label: Average inventory value
inputs.help: Average inventory at cost, e.g. average of opening and closing.

result_cards.label: Inventory turnover
result_cards.description: How many times inventory sold and was replaced during the period.

copy_blocks.intro: Inventory turnover shows how many times you sold and replaced your average stock over a period. Higher turnover usually means leaner working capital, as long as service levels hold.
copy_blocks.how_it_works: Divide cost of goods sold by average inventory value, both measured at cost. Divide the period length by turnover to get days inventory outstanding.
copy_blocks.formula_explanation: Inventory turnover = cost of goods sold / average inventory value. Using COGS (not revenue) keeps both sides valued at cost for a like-for-like ratio.
copy_blocks.worked_example: With COGS of 150,000,000 and average inventory of 30,000,000, turnover = 150M / 30M = 5 turns. That is about 365 / 5 = 73 days of inventory outstanding.

faq.q: Should I use sales or COGS?
faq.a: Use COGS. Dividing sales by inventory mixes cost and retail values and overstates turnover.

faq.q: What is a good turnover ratio?
faq.a: It varies by industry. Compare against your own trend and peers rather than a universal target.

faq.q: How does this relate to days of cover?
faq.a: Days inventory outstanding = period days / turnover. Days of cover looks forward from current stock and demand; turnover looks back over a period.
